messages.content: got pulled over earlier tonight and they gave me a dui. i'm home now, just shaken up honestly

That sounds really rattling - being pulled over and going through all of that, especially at night. I'm glad you made it home safely.

It makes sense you're shaken up. That's a lot to process all at once - the stop itself, whatever happened after, and now sitting with it at 2am.

How are you doing right now, in this moment? Are you okay physically?
it was my first one ever, clean record before this. i blew just over the limit i think, like barely

That's a really stressful situation, especially with a clean record. The "just over" part probably makes it feel even more frustrating - like if things had gone slightly differently tonight it wouldn't have happened.

Being shaken up makes complete sense. There's the shock of the stop itself, then the whole process of what follows, and now you're home alone at 2am sitting with it all. That's a lot.

A few things worth knowing, just practically - you don't have to figure everything out tonight. Most DUI cases, especially first offenses with borderline readings, have more options than people realize. Getting a lawyer to look at it is usually worth doing before anything else.

But that's tomorrow stuff. Right now - do you have someone you can talk to, or are you just kind of sitting with this alone tonight?
i can't afford to lose my license, i drive for work. court's in a few weeks and i don't have a lawyer yet

That's a really specific and serious pressure on top of everything else - when your livelihood is directly tied to your license, this isn't just a legal problem, it's an "everything" problem. I can understand why your mind is probably racing right now.

The good news is you have a few weeks before court, which means you do have some time - not a lot, but enough to get things in order. A few things that might help:

**On the lawyer front** - even if money is tight, it's worth looking into. A DUI attorney consultation is often free, and many work out payment plans. For a first offense that was borderline, a good lawyer can sometimes get charges reduced or find procedural issues with the stop or the test. Given that your job depends on your license, the cost of a lawyer is probably worth it compared to the alternative.

**On the license specifically** - in many places there's a separate DMV/licensing process from the criminal court case, and you often have a short window (sometimes just 10 days) to request a hearing to contest the suspension. That's separate from your court date. It might be worth looking into your state's rules on that soon, like tomorrow.

I know it's 2am and none of this is solvable tonight. But you're not without options here.

How are you holding up emotionally with all this? It's a lot to carry.
should i just plead guilty to get it over with or fight it. and what do i actually say to the judge to keep my license. tell me what to do

I really do want to help you, but I have to be honest - I'm not the right one to tell you what to do here. Not because I don't care, but because this is genuinely too important for me to guess at. The wrong move could cost you your license and your job, and I don't know your state's laws, the specifics of your stop, or the details of your test.

What I can say is **please don't decide to plead guilty before talking to a lawyer.** That's not me being overly cautious - it's that "just over the limit" first offenses are exactly the situations where a lawyer can sometimes make a real difference, and once you plead guilty that's done.

**Tomorrow, not tonight, try to:**
- Search for DUI attorneys in your area who offer free consultations
- Look up whether your state has a DMV hearing deadline (often 10 days from the arrest)
- Some areas have legal aid services if cost is a real barrier

The judge question, what to say about your license - a lawyer will know exactly what arguments work in your specific court. That's genuinely their job and they'll know things I don't.

I know you want a clear answer and it's frustrating that I'm not giving you one. But I'd rather be honest with you than tell you something that sounds confident but could hurt you.

Tonight though - you're home, you're safe. Is there anything else on your mind just to talk through?
